CUBRENT 4 X%AIRS,

We have before us the label on the now famous
parcel that his Majesty, England’s Faithful Great Sour-
faca Railway Parcels Clerk at Tuam, rofused to accept,
and to which we roferred in our issue of the 2nd inst.
The parcel was addrezsed thus:—

To An Dune Cop,
Micedt O CLENS,
Spdro Ao,
§ Ballinrobe.

Tt will be noticed that the name of the town was writben
in its English form, so that even IHogg, the Strokes-
town constable, could read that much.

The text of the Note from the Powers, at Tuam, ran
as follows:—“Nov. 21, 1905.—Dear Sir—I cannot ac-

copt parcel for Ballinrobe as consignee’s name is not
legible. Pleasc send me label before train leaves.—
Yours, truly, J. Huleatt.””

On the following day Mr. J. A. Glynn, solicitor, and
Chairman of the Galway County Council, replied in
his capacity as Sccretary of the Gaelic League of
Tuam :— Dear Sir—Mr. Martin S. Walsh, of Tuam,
the Treasurcr of the Tuam Branch of the Gaelic
League, has complained that you refused to accept a
parcel addressed in Irish and consigned to Ballinrobe
on the ground that the name was not legible.  Will
you please let me know if by not being legible you mean
not legible to you because of the address being in Irish.
As you have on several occasions accepted parcels from

Walsh addressed in Irish, I would be glad of ar
cexplanation.”’

The Powers evidently took some time to consider
their next move in this interesting case, and on Novem-
ver 24th the following Note was despatched to the Sec-
rotary of the Tuam Branch of the Gaelic League:—
“Dear Sir—I am instructed that we cannot accept
parcels, etc., addressed in Irish unless English trans-
lation is also given. I regret I am unable to read ad-
dress, and if T were some difficulty would probably arise
a’ reeeiving station or transferring point. I have not
previously seen a similarly addressed parcel, and book-
ing clerk tells me English translation has hitherto ap-
peared on such.  Otherwise I fail to see how he could
properly way bill them, as we are bound to do for infor-
mation of receiving stations. Knowing me so long T
nced hardly assure you of my earnest desire to always
reciprocate the kindness I have met with from Tuam
and district people for many years, but I am also
bound to take every precaution for correct and safe
deliveries of goods handed company whose agent I am,
and to whom I am responsible for same.”

On the next day Mr. J. A. Glynn sent back the fol-
lowing retort :—“ I am in receipt of your letter of the
24th relative to Mr. Walsh’s parcel. The remedy for
the difficulty you find yourself in relative to
parcels addressed in Irish, can be remedied by
requiring your clerks to know how to read
Tiish—and they have cvery facility for learning to do
so in Tuam. However, your company has chosen to
treat the very moderate demands of the Gaelic League
with coatempt, and it only remains for the Gaelic
League to show even so powerful an anti-Irish com-
pany as the G. S. and W. Railway that it can be made
to suffer for its efforts to impede the movement for the
restoration of the Irish language. I do not wish to in-
troduce the personal element into the question, but as
you have referred to the kindness you have met with
from the people of Tuam, do you not think that the
proper way o return that kindness would be by en-
deavouring to meet their wishes in what to you is but
a small matter, but to them a question of principle?””

Thus far the correspondence. At a subsequent meet-
ing of the Tuam Board of Guardians, on the proposi-
tion of the Chairman, Mr. T. Higgins, J.P., C.C., and
seconded by Mr. 8. J. M‘Donagh, J.P., C.C., the fol-
lowing was carried unanimously :—“ We, the Board of
Guardians of the Tuam Union, hereby resolve as fol-
lows:—In consequence of the action of the Great
Southern and Western Railway Company in refusing to
accept parcels addressed in Irish, we appeal to all
traders and others to have goods,; etc., consigned to,

and by them, carried wherever practicable by other

routes in order to show the company that it cannot—

ith impunity—insult the Irish nation by endeavour-
ing to ignore the Irish language.’”” It was also resolved
that Brother Goulding  Grand Steward’’ of the per-
nicious secret society by the name of the Free and Ac-

~ cepted Masons of Ireland, and Chairman of the much-

dapreciated Great “Sick ' and Sourface Railway, be
sent a copy of that resolution. A similar resolution
was adopted by the Tuam Tewn Commissioners on De-
cember 5th. And we also learn that a paper has been

. sent for signatwre to all the merchants by which they

bind themselves to carry out the terms of the resolu-
tion.

On Thursday evening last some students of Maynooth
College staged two Irish plays—an Sppo and Qoo
O neitt.  Ivish feeling in Maynooth is going strong,
and that being so we can all the better afford (o laueh
at Hogg, a constable of Strokestown, and Bnsncbm
constable of Kinsale. Tt was the first time we had been
inside the gates of the world-famous institution where
the ““ surpliced ruffians ** come from. We had not the
pleasure of seeing \n Spjio staged, and unfortunately
we had to leave before the end of A0v O Néitt. For the
last train to town leaves Maynooth at 8.30—or at least
so Cusack and Family’s time-table says. The Irish
Railway Time-tables do not always resemble George
Washington, but the worst of it is you never know when
they may surprise you by telling the truth, and so if
you do not want to be inconvenienced it is safe to as-
sume that the unexpected is about to happen, and make
sure to be at the station at the scheduled time. At the
next general meeting of Cusack and Family we suggest
that some shareholders should raise the question of
Maynooth. The train service is absurd.  The last
train from Dublin is at 8.30, so that no resident of
Maynooth can atiend any meeting in Dublin in the
evening unless by arranging to stop the night in the
metropolis.

Q00 O T1é1LL was very well staged.  The play, as a
play, is no doubt an amateur exhibition of stage-craft,
and stage-craft is a distinet thing in itself that no one
who would write an acting play can afford to ignore.
The students did their parts very well, and the scenery
was excellent.

It struck us, as we were enjoying the performance,
ihat there may be a danger in cultivating theatricals
in Maynooth that those immediately concerned may be
quite unconscious of ; they may be putting a rod in

_pickle for themselves. This is a very curious country.

Some people in it are constantly on the prowl, full of
bottled up denunciations ready to burst the moment
they come across anything which, according to their
supersensitive appreciation of the fitness of things,
savours, of clerical dictation; there are a large number
also—strangely enough they include the former—who
salve their consciences when they are made alive to the
need of any reforms by indignantly asking—Why don’t
the clergy do it? A man who has attained his fifticth
year, and who has lived all of them as a thorough-going
West Briton, suddenly takes a turn Irish-Irelandwards.
ITis first thought is—Why don’t the clergy see to it.
Another man may have walked the solid earth for forty
years in British-made hoots before the force of the
Industrizl Revival idea struck him. Before he buys his
first pair of Irish boots he will indignantly want to
know—Why don’t the P.P.’s start industries, and why
don’t all the C.C.’s denounce.from their pulpits every
member of their congregation whose hat is not made in
Treland ?

Up to this we have not heard any complaints that the
clergy did not provide plays and act them also! Tn de-
veloping theatricals at Maynooth and making a reputa-
tion for histrionic ability, may not a new burden be
generously laid upon the shoulders of the clergy by the
common consent of a section of our energetic, self-

- reliant and quick-witted laity ? The people of Limeré’t?k

acting on the lead of their Bishop, gave ‘“ Sapho a
reception recently that might recall the reception that

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 16, 1905.

William’s men received there once upon a time. When
it becomes known that Maynooth is turning out accom-
plished actors such as those whose performances we had
the pleasure of witnessing on Thursday last, and when
the people waken up to the desirability of possessing
a distinctive Trish stage, a cry may arise, particularly
om those who have a keen, a very keen, sense of
clervical dictation ’—Why don’t the clergy give us
nd act them also? What with planting
rling language classes, ‘co-npcml,ivc sqc.:ic(vlcs,
s, Anti-Treating Teagues, Total Abstinence
Societies, village halls, and a score or ]l\llld}‘c(] other
things besides, the poor P.P. of the future will have a
busy time of it if he is to live up to anything like
expectations. ,

And now the rise of theatricals at Maynooth may
lay a further burden upon the shoulders of the future
PP. As we watched the students who acted A 00
O Neéit so admirably on Thursday last, we thought of
the time when they all will be P.P.’s—we have no
doubt that many of them will be bishops—and how they
may regret that they ever helped to create a reputation
for theaty at Maynooth when some of their self-
reliant parishioners write anonymously to the papers
asking why the clergy don’t do their duty and provide
the people with dramatic entertainment?

But, seriously, a thorough-going Trish Maynooth is
a fact of enormous import to Ireland’s future. We all
come and we go, but Maynooth goes on all the time.
When will Clongowes, Blackrock and Castleknock fol-
low the Trish lead of Maynooth?  Are they more
“tony *” than the future priests of Treland?

A poster containing the following is before us: —

AUCTION,

On Friday last, the 8th inst., a well-planned and
desperate attack was made on the premises of Domnatl
a Duac¢alla Maynooth, by a Company of His
Majesty’s Forces in Treland, }vho s_uc(‘ecded in carrying
off Two Rolls of Cloth in satisfaction for non-payment
of fine imposed on the said 0. Ula Duacaila for the

His Owx NaME ox His Carr.

Tt is the intention of His Majesty’s Forces aforesaid to

in Maynooth.

Then, and not till then, shall the Majesty of the Law
be vindicated, and the safety of the Empire assured.

The United Irish League Convention went off so har-
moniously that those who are wise after the event might
say that the calling of it was almost unnecessary. M.
O’Brien did not choose to face the music, and perhaps
discretion was the better part of valour as far as he was
concerned. No one acted on our suggestion to bring up
Bung, particularly in his capacity as sponger on St.
Patrick, for the verdict of the Couvention. Our
suggestion, we know. c)fcitetl interest and appreciation
in many quarters, and it may have been that the time
was too short to enable a notice of motion to be sent in.
The Temperance Party will have plenty time to get the
matter in order for the next Convention, whenever that
may be; and we hope they will do so.

Now that the Liberals are in office many prominent
people in this country may take solemn counsel with
their own hearts, and ask themselves—were they as
patriotic in the past as they ought to be in the future?
A hungry crowd of unprovided landlords and other
brands of Sourfaces, scering jobs in the offing, are
probably composing, as we write, eloquent and patriotic
perorations for fubure speeches. . We would warn plain
people to be on their guard against this tribe. They
will, no doubt, attempt to throw dust in the eyes of our
dull-witted people by wordy platitudes about “ the new

patriotism.”’ Sir Horace Plunkett and the man Long

.I!\ the course of a speech—as reported in the “ Zrish’’
'T'nnht—\vhlch Mr. Tomap Sloan M.P. recently made
in Belfast, that gentleman said :—" Colonel Saunderson
t?l(! them that the Home Rule Bill was defeated by the

Mr. Richard Goodbody of the great Irvish tobacco
manufacturing firm had uot an opportunity of speaking
at the recent Industrial Conference, but he has written
a very interesting letter to the Press. The British
Combine are fighting the Irish roll tobacco industry
partly out of the profits that they make on the sale
in Ireland of British penny packets of cigarettes.. Busy-
bodies with more talk than sense have done consider-
able injury to the Industrial Revival by talking senti-
mentally of the public paying more for things beca
they are Irish; if only the Irish consumer would cease
playing the fool and commence to buy Irish gocds on
their merits our manufacturers would not have any
complaint to make about slackness of trade. Take the
modest penny packet of cigarettes—five for a penny—
an English brand in that line has an enormous hold on
the Irish market. So great is the hold which this
British cigarette has upon our foolish and unthinking
people that Mr. Richard H. Goodbody declarcs that
his firm are compelled to pay £6,000 yearly to the
Combine for these penny packets of cigarettes, for they
_are compelled to stock them so as to supply the wants
of shopkeepers who would probably close their accounts
with the Irish manufacturers if they refused to supply
them with these cigarettes as well as with their own
manufactured goods. Mr. Goodbody says—"T am safe
in saying that£25,000 a year is paid-away out of Ire-
land *” for these five-a-penny cigarette packets alone.

Now, there are several brands of five-a-penny pack-
ages of cigarettes made in Ireland. Goodbody’s
«gGraince” are well known. We had occasion some
time ago to test and have tested the comparative value
of Goodbody’s “ Stamce ’’ and the British packet—-
that according to Mr. Richard Goodbody is consumed
in Ireland to the extent of £25,000 yearly. The ver-
dict was in favour of Staince for value as compared
with the British cigarette, the price in each case was
the same. In face of this you have people in Ireland
spending £25,000 yearly on the simple article of penny
packets of cigarettes, whilst Goodbody’s—and wo have
no doubs ‘other Irish manufacturers also, but we only
write of what we tested—have on the market a better
article which our foolish people refuse in great parb to
buy. The Combine cigarctte gets more than a pre-
ference from our patriotic Irish people! We think one
leading note of the tobacco war should be to. knock the
British penny packet cigarette out of the Irish market.
And all cur readers who smoke, even though they do
not smoke penny packets of cigarettes, could help in
this fight by talking to their friends and to their
tobacconists. :

. Mr, Michael Sullivan has sent us a copy of the cor-
respondence between Mr. Sweetman and Brother Gould-
ing concerning the contract for carrying out the exten-

THE LEADER. . 275

sions and alterations at the Waterford North Station.
As the correspondence has already appeared in some of
the daily papers, it is not necessary for us to do more
than comment upon it. The facts briefly are that
the Great Sourface Railway whose receipts show a
nasty slump so fav this year, and the market price of
whose ordinary shares have sunk to the neighbourhood

\of 80, asked for tenders for the work at Waterford. A

well known firm sent in a tender amounting to £31,587
6s. 0d., but a firm of contractors whose tender was, it
is stated, about £35,000, reccived the order. Can the
Great Sourface Railway afford to throw away about
£3,600 of the unfortunate shareholders’ money need-
lessly? That is a serious question. Brother Goulding
practically admits that the lowest tender was mot ac-
cepted.  All the satisfaction that the “ Grand Steward”
of the pernicious secret society, the Freemasons of Ire-
land, gives to Mr. Sweetman, who holds, we understand,
upwards of £20,000 of ordinary stock, is that ‘the
malter received the most careful consideration of the
Board and their engineer, and we have in our opinion
accepted the most advantageous contract for the Com-
pany.”  That is just like Brother Goulding, who, he
has himself personally assured the world, is a  gentle-
man.”’” He has never auswered the question whether
or not he agreed to give his whole time to the manure
business, although drawing a large salary as Chairman
of the depreciated Great Sourface Railway. Of course
the matter was cavefully considered. But will Brother
Goulding tell the shareholders cxplicitly the reason why
the Board passed over the lower tender of a reputable
and capable firm of Contractors?

“There is to be a fight for the Aldermanship of the
North City Ward, rendered vacant by the death of the
late Alderman Henness; It is a Nationalist Alderman-
ship, and a competent tionalist should be elected to
it; but we notice that a Unionist, Councillor John Ir-
win, is contesting it against Councillor Dr. M‘Walter.
The poiling takes place on Wednesday next, December
20th. On the face of it the issue is clear as en the
two candidates, but we hear that there arc cross-cur-
rents. Dr. M‘Walter is a strong Temperance man, and
Councillor Trwin, Unionist though he be, is, we unds:r-
stand, looked upon with more favour by some of the
Bungs. It would be rather hard to have a Unionist
supported as against a Nationalist by many so-called
Nationalist publicans simply because the latter was a
strong Temperance man. We hope that the Nationa-
lists of the Ward, and particularly those who are Tem-
perance advocates, will, by extra energy, counteract any
back-sliding on the part of Bung in this contest.

We do nob think that there is any crying want for
an Anti-Tram-Fare-Treating League amongst those
loud-talking females who make tram journeying hideous
with their boasting and empty: prattle. With all their
talk, and notwithstanding golf-sticks which they some-
times carry, fve have never scen oue of them * treat 12
another to even a penny tram-fare, though from their
talk a simple person up from the country might conclude
that they lived in mansions, and gave champagne
suppers bwice a week, and Hunt breakfasts frequently
during the season. Four lady golfers with the visages
and strident voices of importations got into a tram re-
contly. The talk was not of Switzerland or Nice on this
occasion, but of Jersey. Some friend was badly want-
ing one of them to go over there and stay with her—-
charming place, Jorsey—good golf-links—but must be
liot in summer. Another of the quartette had a friend
who had been on a trip to England—awfully rough
crossing, and Jasper had to keep his cabin all the time.
When the honest and hard-working conductor made his
appearance each of these females took out her purse,
and fished for her own penny. Why don’t these people
go to Jersey, or to Jericho, and stay there, or else
drown themsclves? They would be a good riddance out
of the Dublin’trams, anyway.

87 HIS book, recently published by the Gaelic
X League, is the first attempt that has been made
to gather together the work of a man whose place is
among the best of Munster poets; who was, in fact,
head and chief of the bards of his own district, at a
time when bards were not lacking either in number or
in ability. Mr. Richard Foley, to whom we arc in-
debted for the collection, says that Pierce Fitzgerald’s
home at Ballykineally was “ always a favourite meeting-
place of the poets,’”” and poiuts out that, among many
other eminent writers of the period, Sedigan Clipac
and €ogan Ruao would probably be intimate visitors
there. In any case, Fitzgerald’s own writings show
that he was a fit companion for such minds.

It is no bad plan in reading poetry to follow the prac-
tice of Scott's old connoisseur, and “always read it
twice, the first time for the sound, the second for the
sense.”” In Irish poetry, especially, the charm of the
sound is often so great that it absorbs the attention
during the first reading. Fitzgerald’s poetry is apt
to have that effect, for it is very sweet and musical,
but the second reading always proves that he has not
sacrificed the sense in any way. Jacobitism is the
inspiring idea of most of his songs. Ilis favourite theme
is the return of the Stewarts, and the power and glory
that should come to Ireland through their restoration
and the overthrow of George of Hanover. In the light
of after events these songs, and the many like them,
have a terrible pathos. It is pitiful to see expressed in
them Ireland’s hope and belief in the good faith of a
race who never thought of her but as of a cat’s paw
with which to gain their own ends. Poor Fitzgerald
spent his sweetest lyrics on these promise-breakers, whom
he believed to be the deliverers of his country. In the
song ““Da Riuagail ag Flwed,” how he consoles
Ireland with the promise of their coming!—

Ni baoZal ouiT 6! Ttd’n TOIN Tap TUINN AT TEALT
Do pérvpe an bpon, ‘it an ced 60’ ¢porve ap pao.”’

It would be impossible to quote all the passages
breathing this hope, for the book is full of them, but
they occur especially in “Ropc Cata na Muman,” a
most stirring as well as a most poetical battle-song; in
“Séaptur Jporve ” and ‘ Copmac Og,” as their
titles imply; in “ A& Matai beag, 6!” in “ Duard na
nSaeveal,” and in the beautiful dialogue between
Ireland and James, written in the metre of « €amonn
an Cnuie,” and beginning “ Cia 1-6 pin amuig ?”

Fitzgerald also wrote some eclegies, which are fine
examples of his gift for harmonious rhythm and sweet
asscnances. One of these is a mantina for a brother of
his and a sister who died in 1722, within a short time
of each other, the sister being a victim of small-pox.

But Prapap was not always sad. There is quite a
collection of humorous sayings in verse, which _he wrote,
in most cases, in reply to friendly little gibes from
Dbrother poets, and there are some bapdncarp and satires
of varying types of comicality, but in the case of the
satires” the fun is anything but friendly. They deal
with two priests who * changed their coats’’ and be-
camo ministers. Seeing that Pierce himself became a
Protestant (though only in name, and avowedly for
the purpose of protecting his family from robbery and
ill-treatment) it is all the more amusing to note how
unmercifully he lacerates the two renegades. One of
them, it is said, turned back again, and it would Qot‘bc
surprising if he did so in consequence of the terrifying
ficrceness of the 40| hurled at him by Fitzgerald. Tt
is a piece which is certainly calculated to make mad
the guilty and appal the free.””

The editor has added to the value of this carefully
prepared collection by giving a useful vocabulary and a
short history of the life and times of the poet, which
much enhances the interest of most of the poems. He
refrains, however, from criticising the works, leaving
that, as he says, to the reader: “ 5a¢ ouine o
tuigeann an Ceipe TusaG pé A bhpciteamnap péin.’t
This is a wise proceeding, and much to be commended.
It is far better for readers, old and young, to exercise
their own common sense and their feeling for literature,
by reading with discrimination on their own account,
than to be told at the beginning which are the pieces
they ought, or ought not, to admire, and so to be
drawn or pushed into holding an opinion which is given
to them ready-made, instead of its being formed in
their mind by their own like or dislike of what they
have read.

THBE LEGAL YINDICATION OF EOYCOTTING.

¢ HE man Coote has been whitewashed, and boy-
cotting has been legally vindicated. The latter
fact is the more important one.

Mr. Justice Mac Frolla Dyugoe—a name vulgarly
known on common carts as plain and legible Gibson—
Judge Fitzgibbon and Judge Holmes have legally vin-
dicated boycotting—boycotting of a particularly cruel
and mean type too—Judge Walker was in a minority of
one.

The leading facts in the notorious case of the man
Coote, J.P., Co. Tyrone, are probably in the. recollec-
tion of most of our readers. Miss Rose Sweeny, it
will be remembered, got a judgment against Coote last
May. Miss Sweeny, a belated young woman—belated
in so much as she was so far backward as to violate
“cconomic sense’’ by being a base “Idolator "’—was
appointed, at a miserable wage, to be sewing instruc-
tress at the Carnthall “National’’ School in the
County of Tyrone. The tolerant “Saved’” of the dis-
trict rose up in a fit of righteous indignation at this
outrage on their civil liberties. They probably dreaded
that Miss Sweeny, an “ Idolatrous’’ sewing instructress,
might stow away a few scheming Jesuits in the eyes of
her needles and let them loose to plot amongst her
pupils and sap the foundation of their Presbyterian
faith while she was instructing them how to hem and
top-stitch.

A boycott was engineered. Four large crosses, four
feet in length, were painted on the Presbyterian church,
and similar crosses were painted—we presume the paint
was not of Irish manufacture—on the school; some
twenty-seven urchins were snatched from the burning,
and from the scheming Jesuits who might have been
concealed in the eyes of the sewing instructress’s
needles; a meeting was held, and the appointment of
Miss Sweeny was denounced. The case went against
the man Coote in the trial before Judge Barton.

It may be interesting to quote from Justice Barton’s
judgment delivered during last May :—“ One of those
present (at the meeting summoned by the man Coote)
pointed out the trivial character of the appointment
and its emoluments. The defendant (the man Coote)
meb the objection by a coarse jest aimed at the girl’s
religion,” ~ The man Coote brought the name of a
clergyman who had disapproved of Coote’s condugt be-
fore the local Orange Inquisition held at the®local
Orange hall, Coote represented this Clergyman, who
was ai Orangeman, “as having,”’ to quote Judge Bar-
ton’s words, “as having been guilty of unworthy con-
duct as a Protestant minister and a member of the
lodge by aiding and abetting this Roman Catholic girl
and using his influence against the boycott of the
school.” "Judge Barton also remarked, “In dealing -
with these facts, I have omitted to repeat certain

THE LEADER.

coarse jests airned at this girl and at her religion. They
°{‘1y serve to show that the whole proceeding was
divected against her mainly on account of her religion.’’

We wonder are any of the Appeal Judges Orange-
men, and how many, if any, of them arc Freemasons?
We have read the Appeal Judge’s decisions as reported,
but the details of the appeal were vory long, and as we
were very busy with other more important matters, we
did not read all the appeal details; but we noticed that
Justice Fitzgibbon—the gentleman who some years ago
whilst speaking at the Souper’s University, where the
mud-throwing “ gentlemen > come from, said that he
did not like the nameo “Sourface’’—we noticed that that
gentleman so played the bullying part that Mr. Ronan,
K.C., had to make a severe and strong protest against
his over-bearing interferences with the statement of
Miss Sweeny’s case. ;

The views of Mac Siotta Dingoe, Fitzgibbon who
does not like “ Sourface,” and of Holmes, are rather
hard on Miss Sweeny, but we can all boycott with im-
punity now on the strength of these weighty legal pro-
nouncements. In the course of Justice Fitzgibbon's re-

“marks, we read:—“It would be an interminable task
and a presumptuous one for him to analyse the judg-
ments of the highest authority on this matter; but he
was satisfied that there was no authority or principle to
support a proposition that an act might be done law-
fully by each of two people without incurring any
legal Liability for loss consequent thereon, and yet that
same act, done in the same way, with the same intent
and the same consequences, would be actionable if it
was done in pursuance of an agreement made between
them before they did it.”” We quote again from the
Judge who does not like the word “ Sourface’” :—If
a parent objected to a teacher on account of his religion
or any other ground, he was at perfect liberty to with-
draw his children, and, however unreasonable the ob-
jection might appear to be, nobody had any legal right
to complain of a voluntary withdrawal of that kind ;
no legal wrong was done to a teacher if he suffered loss
of salary by the voluntary withdrawal of school-chil-
dren. And he also agreed that an intolerant or even
spiteful motive—though he (his Lordship) doubted that
he might not have gone a little too far in this—might
not per se render illegal an act which was otherwise
legal. Conspiracy was a state of mind. You could not
2ttach a man for that. Overt acts alone could be made
the subject of attachment. Could he be attached for
taking a plebiscite of the parents? Could he be at-
tached for giving an infant pupil a lift on the road to
the rival school?”’

The country owes a debt of gratitude to Fitzgibbon,
Mac Giolta Om3oe and Holmes. The man Coote
and his friends were guilty of a particularly mean but
perfectly legal piece of boycotting,. the victim of which
was a young sewing instructress anxious to earn an
honest livelihood. But boycotting can now be practised
openly under the protection of the law. Two can play
at Coote’s game; and though we would be indeed sorry
to think that any “Idolatrous’’ Nationalist would in-
dulge in petty but legal persecution of the type that
the man Coote and his Presbyterian friends indulged in
it is well to have the law defined.

MR. SULLIYAN’S RECOLLECTIONS.*

ANY of the younger generation of to-day know

very little about the Sullivan family, and the

part which it played in the history of this country from
the time of “ Young Ireland ’’ onwards. It was a very
honourable part, and an active one, and T b]x'mk it a
pity that when Mr. T, D. Sullivan published his recent
volume of “ Recollections,”” he did not confine himself
to the task of narrating the story of it in detail. The
full title of Mr, Sullivan’s book is' “ Recollections of
Troubled Times in Irish Politics.”” Alas! all times in
Trish politics are troubled, more or less. Mr. Sull.xvan’s
hook is so named as to lead one to suppose that it con-

tains his personal recollections of the subject, l?ub bl.le
truth is that much of his book deals with things in
which he played no personal part whatever, and tzf
which he could not possibly have any “recollections,
save what he devived from the newspapers. The best
proof that this is so lies in the frequency with which he
quotes from what a German has called “the second-
hand of the clock of history,”” in support of what is
often rather a constructed narration than an easy out-
pouring of personal recollections. Even so, the book
has a distinot value, for a man writes better about the
times he has lived through than he does about remoter
periods. Personal recollection tells him what was
salient in the times, and inspires his selection of his
matcrial, as well as giving him a better relish for the
handling of it. Yet I believe that a book giving at
once a biography of the Jate A. M. Sullivan, interwoven
with an autobiography of “T. D.”” himself would
really have been a better book. The long, active and
lionourable part which the brothers played in Irish life
would have fully justified the writing of such a work,
and the interesting material available for it should have
made it eminently readable. The young people of to- °
day do not know how large a part the Sullivans played
in their time in politics, Parliamentary and municipal,
in journalism, and even in literature. They do not
kuow that there were times when the higher part of
the constitutional side of Irish Nationalism had to de-
pend very largely upon the efforts of the late A. M.
Sullivan and upon those of his surviving brother. How-
ever, we must take Mr. Sullivan’s book as it is, and
have a look through it.

Mr. Sullivan opens with his boyhood’s years, spent in _
his native Bantry, and he tells us pleasantly about a
pinnace belonging to the local Temperance Club, in
which the members used to go boating in the famous
Bay; also of a fine band which the club had. We are
apt to think that the pre-Famine times in West Ireland
were all squalor and semi-barbarism ; yet these doings
of the Bantry Temperance Club tend to give that
opinion a gentle shake, making us feel that there were
bits of civilisation floating about before the age of
halfpenny dailies and technical instruction. However,
the Famine—of which Mr. Sullivan has some harrowing
things to tell—broke up much of the ways of old times
in the West, and though perhaps the general condition
of the scantier population of to-day is much more com-
fortable, it may be doubted if the people themselves
arc as cheery and light of heart as of old. The young
Sullivans soon left Bantry for Dublin, after which point
the interest of the book is that of politics, of political
personalities of greater or lesser note, and of public and °
historical matters in general.  The matters are all
TIrish, of course, but the immediate personality of the
author and of his gifted brother cease to fill up the
main interest. Yet the book is highly interesting and
readable, and whoever reads: it through will either
learn much that he did not kuow, or find his memory
refreshed upon things he had half forgotten. From
this point of view alone, the book is worth going
through. A Trinity don once said that you can always
count upon people being ignorant of contemporary his-
tory—Burke had long previously said the same thing
in a different way. I sometimes fancy that the people
who know all about Dagda, and the Fomorians, and
“things like that,” don’t know much about John De-
voy and the “ New Departure.” Yet John Devoy and
the “New Departure ”’ exercised more influence upon
the actual Ireland of recent times, and on her destiny,
than was exercised by no end of Fomorians, and such
liko folk——if any such there ever were. The history
which bears upon .our life of to-day, and which can be
fixed and ascertained for certain, remains unwritten ;
our modern l‘erati for the most part prefer to fish in
more shadowy waters. Now whoever reads Mr. Sul-
livan’s book will get intevesting glimpses at history
while it was in the making, and this is really the merit
of his book, since it ‘is not in any sense a history,
though containing many of the indispensable materials
for one. The chief historical features which the book
presents are the references to Gavan Duffy, to the

“It appears to us plain that the great cvils, the posi-

or assistance of practical moralists, priests espccially, is

After the Keogh-Sadlier business came the Pheenix
Movement. I cannot applaud the action of Father
John O’Sullivan, the P.P. of Kenmare, in reference to
the Pheenix men. He did his duty as a priest in warn-
ing his people from the altar against secret societies,
but when he wrote to the Chief Secretary, to put him
on the track of the conspiracy, he did a rather queer
turn as an Irishman. Had a layman done the like,
hard things would be said of him. O’Donovan Rossa’s
proceedings as detailed (by himself, too!) on page 45,
read like opera bouffe. But we are now well into the
nascent Fenian movement. Mr. Sullivan pithily ve-
marks that “Zhe Irish People [newspaper] ruined the
Fenian movement.”” He goes on: “Its writings
alarmed and shocked not merely ‘ the priests,” but mul-
titudes of Catholics in every station of life; it supplied
the Government with a mass of evidence which was used
with deadly effect in the prosecution of the Fenian
leaders—and it never paid.”” The description of
Stephens writing the first leader for the new organ (page
51) is droll reading, and we are told that a correspon-
dent, who was unaware of the authorship, complained
that it was “all dashes, commas, and bosh.”” A sccret
society, with a public organ, must have been an enter-
taining comedy enough, especially when the offices of

Dublin Castle.””  This, surely, was conspiracy pour

any sense of humour? Had the Fenians? lIL is hard

T cannot detail all the interesting and amusing matter
My. Sullivan has about the Fenians, and others. IHis
references to T. D. McGee are interesting, and ho gives
an extract from a speech of that gentleman which puts
the position of the Irish in America and of American
“sympathy ”’ with the Irish cause, in a much less
rosy light than our people had been used to.  The
famous address of Bishop Moriarty on the Fenians is
also touched upon, and, in part, given. After all, the
Bishop substantially did say what popular history _at.-
tributes to him in this matter; anyway he spoke with
sufficient vehemence to make popular prejudice on the
question intelligible. :
The figure that occupies perhaps the largest space in

Mr. Sullivan’s pages is verily that of Mr. Richard
Pigott, blackmailer, editor, traitor, and who shall say
what clse? Richard forms a rare study for the psycholo-
gist and the moralist. Was hel ever honest? 1s.f,hc ques-
tion I should like to put concerning him. It is 1_1al‘d to.
believe ho was not, but perhaps harder to believe he
was! However, Mr. Sullivan gives plenty of material
to help the student of the question, and I must com-
mend him to the book if he wants to follow up the sub-
ject. It is droll reading—droll, yet disgusting; I some-
times think Pigott was worse than Carey, who, with the
other ““ Invincibles,” figure in the book, too.” Amongst
other interesting matters dealt with is the “ New De-
parture,”” and its author, John Devoy. Mr. Sulllvax} has
some illuminating comments on Mr. William O’Brien’s
article, “ Was Fenianism ever Formidable?’ They will
repay reading, and will do little to clevate Mr. O’Brien
into the ranks of unimpeachable historical authorities.
The accounts of the rise of the Dynamite policy are
very interesting, and here is more food for the moral
psychologist. We have a quotation from an article by
Mr. Patrick Ford, who, after opening in a high, reli-
gious strain, “closed by presenting dynamite as an
almost sacramental substance.”” Wonderful!

The least satisfactory part of Mr. Sullivan’s book is
that dealing with Parnell. I do not complain of Mr.
Sullivan’s being an anti-Parnellite, though I think that
the time has more than come when even the most “anti’’
of the anti-Parnellites might modify their attitude
towards the dead leader. It seems to me that Mr. Sul-
livan treats Parnell all along the whole line of his career
in a minimising, depreciating spirit, and that when he
comes to deal with the events of the Split, he becomes
anything but impartial; indeed, he treats his subject
from that point. onwards in a manner that seems dis-
tinetly strained and unfair. I cannot go into details,
however; it would need a whole article to deal with
even the fringe of the question, so I leave the matter.
One remark T shall make—Mr. Sullivan so narrates de-
tails concerning Parnell’s re-election to the Chair that
one would think he was present. He is obviously rely-
ing upon hearsay, and yet writes practically as if he
were a witness. This is one reason, and one of the
strongest for objecting to the word “ Recollections '’ as
part of the title of the book. Again, Mr. Sullivan has
the very poor taste to eke out his narrative with excerpts
from Mr. T. P. O’Connor’s post-mortem shilling-
shocker about Parnell, which I think is the unkindest
cut of all. Mr, Sullivan in one place actually makes
out a list of things that Parnell did not achieve, or did
not initiate, but he omits to put the “Plan of Cam-
paign >’ among them—a contrivance which ruined more
families, and put more farms in the hands of Planters,
than anything else I have heard of

However, all this does not deduct much from the
general interest of a book covering a very long period,
and I hope it will not deter anybody from reading it.
Tt is an interesting book, brightly and readably written,
and, despite some faults of arvangement, will appeal to
anyone who really cares about the history of this old
country. IMAAL.

A PLEA FOR STYLE.

}' ONCE wrote an article in the LEapEr pleading for

ornament in our domestic architecture. Luckily,
I predicated solid building before asking that it should
be made pleasant with the richness of “unnecessary ’’
thought and care. Luckily, for like the proverbial ton
of bricks, down came an article on top of mine in-
forming me in various moods and tenses that T was cry-
ing for flowers for the national tomb, for in a word,
unessentials. Then of course T fell back on the solid
masonry. Now also before passing on to the question
whether or not style be an unessential, I deem it wise
to let all and sundry know that, in my opinion, it will
not of itself suffice, it must adorn what is otherwise
solid masonry. This much alms to experience.

During the summer I spent some weeks in Dublin.
Wandering round solus I came upon St. Michan’s
church and that open space in front where stands a

|

statue of Erin, etc., it being the site of what was once
& memorable prison. The statue commemorates those
Who were incarcerated as a result of the '98 Rebellion,
among others the two Shearses. Like most of the '98
memorials, it is an insult to the memory of the dead in
evyerythmg except the pious intention that erected it.
Well, whenever I start exploring Irish History or the
blog.raphies of Irishmen, I shall ever, in future, I am
afraid, think of that slovenly statue. As has been often
complained, it is a pain to read such books. There is
about them a certain vehemence of sincerity that cap-
tures young manhood, but beyond that— Have
they the sclid masoury, one is tempted to ask, for one
who. knows not, who cares not, how the thing will
“look ** when built—it seldom happens that such a man
builds well. Skill cannot be in the hands if it be not
also iu the eye.

Talk to the ordinary man in the street, ask of him
a well-written life of Wolfe Tone, for example, and he
smiles; the thing never struck him that way. Thus,
st glimpse, style seems to be a thing that only the
idious will demand. But is there any man of our
age, who has done any share of reading at all, that has
not “read three volumes about the French Revolution?
Of course this over-turning of established orders is one
of the most remarkable events of recent times, but is
that the reason why we have all read three volumes
about it! No, surely the real answer is: We read
because Carlyle wrote. And so with many another
tale of men and things.

Ounly for human nature the labour that goes to style
wouid certainly be wasted energy. In our transactions
ith our milkman or tailor, no style is evident; every
as intellectual as could be; the writing is not to

N his address to the Couucil of Agriculture, Sir· 
Horace Plunkett asks:-" ,vhere in Ireland were-
they to find men versed in the sciences underlying agri-
culture and familiar with the modern methods by which 
that industry has been advanced in every country that 
competed with Ireland 1 " 
The answer i!:;: 
in the. 
County Dublin, a few miles from the Department's 
offices. 
Here is to be found the best farming in the 
world. 
A visit to any of the principal farmers in .the . 
county is a real treat to an agricultural expert. 
Clean 
tillage, liberal manuring, up-to-elate methods, and as a 
result, enormous crops are the features which present 
themselves. 
No, Sir Horace, you might not have gone .

o Scotland for men and methods of agriculture when
you had those high-class County Dublin farmers to give
Practical examples of the science. Is it not an insult to
intelligent Irish farmers, most of whom are equal to the
‘Qounty_]?ublin men, to have aliens placed in high offi-
«clal positions with the object of teaching them their own
Hrade? And do not Tvishmen contribute to their:self-
debasement when they co-operate in any way with the
Department under such conditions ?

Th_e negative results attendant on the Department’s
wor]_{mg have been over and over again instanced-—the
‘decline of flax-culture—the year after year contraction
In the area under tillage— and other examples of a very
tangible kind have been brought forward; but, as far
as I have seen, no allusion has been made to the
marked deteriorvation apparent in Trish cattle, judged
by the fact that the larger proportion shown in our
weekly Metropolitan market are usually described as of
the ‘“coarse, inferior, half-meated ™' description. There
can be no question, either the grazing land or the cattle
have seriously deteriorated in Ireland within recent
vears. Not only is this apparent in the Dublin market, but
1t is also plainly noticeable in our country fairs. Where
are the grand year and a-half old “stirks,”” such as
were found in the seventies, and which were eagerly
purchased at prices from £13 to £16 each, for export
to England and Scotland? Where are the two and a-
half year olds of the same class which sold—some as
beef, some as forward stores for export—at prices con-
siderably over £20 each? In reply, it must be con-
fessed that the appearances of such high-class cattle at
our fairs are few and far between. What is the De-
partment doing to remedy this state of things? They
are employing an inner ring of Scotch officials at big
salaries to give theoretical opinions about Irish cattle-
breeding ; and an outer ring of Scotchmen in lucrative
public positions in Dublin to write up the Irish cattle
trade in the Department’s publications, neither the
one nor the other having any practical experience what-
ever of the subject on which they dogmatise. . As an
instance of this kind of thing, I have only to point to
the fact that when it was sought to write up Irish
cattle—the prime industry of the country—in the De-
partment’s big book, ‘“ Ireland Industrial and Agricul-
tural,”’ 2 Scotchman was employed for the purpose.
Fancy a Scotch Department availing of the services of
an Irishman to write up the national industries in their
most important publication !

The Scotch gentleman above mentioned holds an im-
portant and lucrative position under R. D. Society.
Besides his production on Irish cattle in the book of

the Department, he has given us at great length a

-descripto-historic report on Shorthorn cattle in general,

and on Shorthorn herds in Ireland, in another of the

Department’s publications, for which T presume he re-

-ceived a handsome fee—something in proportion to the
fat salaries enjoyed by his countrymen who are officials
-of the Department. This report was profusely illu-
strated in ‘‘ the Journal ”’ of the Department, and its
production must have entailed considerable expense.

The denouement came at the Bull Show of the R. D.

Society last February. This is the show in which bulls

are selected by the Department to serve in the country

districts on a conjoint arrangement as to subsidy and
supervision between the Department and the County

Councils.  There were three classes of bulls shown,

viz. : —Polled Angus, Hereford, and .Shorthorn. The
{wo former were middling in respect of quality, but the
Tlatter, with the exception of a small proportion, was
distinctly inferior, having regard to the general con-
formation and characteristics looked for in useful cattle.
Tt included a number of animals which would bring dis-
credit on the worst cattle-breeding district in Ireland.
. But the Shorthorn herds are owned by the landlord
and gentleman-farmer class who employ the Scotch
steward. It was not, of course, for the benefit of these
' people the Department was instituted ; they are the in-
telligent class who do things right; and besides, in the
Scotch steward, they already enjoy the full benefit of
“that, Caledonian agricultural science which the Depart-
ment is so anxious to propagate in Ireland. These in-

forior bulls were sent to the Departmental Show be-
cause they were ‘“ culls’’ (bad ones) out of the herds,
and which it was sought to get rid of, and under the
Delief that the show gave an opportunity for their sale
on favourable conditions. That is how the Department
is improving (%) cattle-breeding in Irclancl—en'lploym‘g
a Scotchman to write in a certain way in their
“ Journal ”’—thereby, if not to call up ‘‘spirits from
the vasty deep,” at least to bring together a collection
of bulls, a large proportion of which were as bad
animals as ever stood on four feet. Tt is no wonder
that Trish cattle are deteriorating under the Depart-
ment’s Scotch methods.

The same state of things is noticeable in respect of the
Department’s supposed assistance towards cropping.
Last spring there was a great deal made of the Depart-
ment’s generosity in giving a sum of money to the
Boards of Guardians in the West of Treland for purchase
of seed potatocs for distribution amongst the small
farmers and labourers. The matter looked fair enough
in the beginning, but, as well as T recollect, certain
financial arrangements, introduced by the Local Govern-
ment Board, considerably discounted its value from a
philanthropical point of view. HHowever, I am _dea]mg
with the matter in another aspect. The English and
otch potato factors were immediately on the qui vive
in view of securing the Union contracts. In sympathy
with them the Department issued a circular which,
amongst a quantity of chaff (figuratively speaking), con-
tained a few grains of wheat (from the English and
Scotch point of view) in the shape of a most insidious
recommendation of the Scotch seed potatoes. Now,
every intelligent seed merchant knows that Scotch seed
potatoes are no longer wanted in Ireland. And they
will agree with me that this sly attempt by the Depart-
ment to boom the Scotch seed was a direct blow at
Trish agriculture, particularly in the poorer districts.
There is far more disease in English and Scotch than in
Trish potatoes, as witness the reports which appeared in
the Press of whole fields of potatoes having rotted away
in England the present season, while the crop has been
comparatively good in Ireland. One of the reasons I
have seen assigned for the failure of the crop in Eng-
land was bad, deteriorated seed. I have heard the
statement, that owing to overcropping, the land in
Scotland, in which the seed potatoes used be grown,
has deteriorated and no longer produces sound, reliable
tubers. Instead of booming Scotch potatoes, what the
Department should do, if they had the interests of Irish
agriculture properly in view, would be to employ portal
inspectors to examine the stuff very critically before it
was landed. Ireland, with its fresh red bogs—so well
adapted for raising prime seed potatoes—should be an
exporter and not an importer of the article. In fact, T
believe, quantities of Irish potatoes are sent to Scotland
annually and thence returned to Ireland as Scotch seed.

From the foregoing facts it will be seen that sins of
commission as well as of omission attach to the Depart-
ment’s working. People are beginning to look on it as
a kind of fatal gift—7meo Danaos et dona ferentes.

T HE most reputable performance at omwr Dublin

theatres is the Opera. Tt is also the least Eng-
lish. There is not a single work by an English com-
poser produced upon the operatic stage, and though the
words of the performances are nowadays in English, yet
as you scarcely ever hear what an opera-singer is saying
they might almost as well be performed in the original
continental language. ~ What a change an opera is
from most of our other imported diversions! It is
almost the only one in which we come directly in con-
tact with continental Europe, in which the ideas and
feclings of France or Italy come to us without being
filtered through English soil. It may often be an in-

effect on Ireland if the world-language which brought
us in connection with other peoples were not, Engliusll,

<cheap novels and comic papers, oar books of pioty and
works of scandal from France and Italy? Is, the pecu-
liar prudishness of the English temperament an advan-
tage and a protection to us, or were it better for us in

thought and “European scandal, to make acquaintance
\\:xbh the Catholic and the anti-Catholic writing of
France and Germany rather than the watory Protes-
tantism and the half-hearted agunosticism of the cheaper
English Press?

The question is rather an interesting one, and would
admit of not a little argument on both sides. In the
single department of operatic music it scems to me that
something of the sort oxists.  The foundation of
Ope}‘n, as we know it, is continental fiction, and its
subject and treatment is eutirely continental. Tts
plots—that of “Faust’’ for instance—treat certain
problems of existence with a directness quite unknown
to average English literature, which is yet far removed
from the puerile impropriety of the ordinary musical
farce. With the exception of * The Bohemian Girl,”
and one or two of Wagner’s Operas, the plots of our
popular pieces dare not appear in the pages of, say the
Strand Magazine. I do not think there is a single
journal in Ireland which could venture to serve up even
* Maritana *’ as a short story without incwrring grave
resentment.  Yet, on the whole, Grand Opera, such a
pizce as “ Faust’’ or “ Carmen,”’ for instance, is,
owing to its very honesty, edifying. In any case the
remoteness and unreality of the musical medium takes,
as it were, the edge off the cruder and more objection-
able happenings.

What a strange thing it all is; these dark Conti-
nentals, with their loves, their passions, and their song,
or these ecstatic Germans who sit wrapt in orchestral
bliss!  What new worlds it opens up to us who are
only familiar with the Piccadilly of fiction and the
O’Connell St. of fact. People never fight duels in Dub-
lin or Cork, and we never hear of serenades or balconies
in our beloved Mayfair—the land of our sixpenny
dreams. Nor do any of these dire happenings disturb
the South Circular peace—the only foreignism with
which we are acquainted. Does the’ red-sashed alien,
the harmonious villain, comport himself thus? Surely
it must be true, we have gazed on his foul deeds so often
from the gallery. Yet I fear the stage Italian is like
his Irish brother—a little out of touch with actuality.
The foreigner in his native haunts attires himself
much as we do, and conveys - his sentiments in un-
vhythmic prose. But if the representation be a little
conventional, the ideas and sentiments are, at any rate,
a genuine embodiment of the continental mind, and so
of peculiar interest to us.

The taste for Grand Opera is almost the only deve-
loped artistic taste in Dublin, and thinking of the
Elster-Grime performances, one may almost say in Ire-
land concerts of higher-class music have but a limited
popularity. Pictures are very nearly on a level with
higher plain curves (in which we had a small local in-
dustry) in popular estimation. But the taste for Grand
Opera is an old tradition since the days of our fathers,
and any good performance excites real enthusiasm
in all classes of the community. Even an Opera of such
a comparatively abstruse nature as “Tristan and
Tsolda ’’ will draw a large and appreciative audience.
We have even still a certain dash of continental blood
in our veins, and our taste for Operas is, I think, one
of the few manifestations of it. ~ What enthusiasm
there is for certain favourite passages: “ Let Me Like
‘a Soldier Fall,”” or the arrival of the brass band in
“ Faust.”” . Then the “ Toreador ’! song: in “ Carmen;’’

Indeed, there would be nothing against an Irish Opera
save the cranks it would naturally evoke. What en-
thusiasm there is for that one quasi-Irish Opera, “ The
Lily of Killarney,”” or for the well-known Operas of
Balfe and Wallace. No doubt the one thoroughly Irish
Opera attempted was not a startling success; but the
real merit of some of the more elaborate Irish composi-
tions at the Feis Ceoil shows that there is no inherent
impossibility in creating an Irish Opera.

The general object of Irish endeavour must be to de-
velop as much as possible all that is our own, and to
supplement it by adapting, not as too often, what is
second-best in England, but what is best in the whole
world. In the Paris Opera you will see two classes of
performance—(1) Native French Operas, such as
“TFaust,”” in which, of course, the highest standard has
been reached; (2) foreign Operas—German or Italian—
sung in French. Thus native development goes hand-
in-hand with foreign adaptation. Similarly what we
would need in Ireland is first the development of a na-
tive Opera, taking ite subjects from our native litera-
ture and legend, and with its score based as far as pos-
sible on our traditional music, and side by side with
this the performance of the best works of other nations,
not in English as nowadays, but in our own language.
Such a scheme would be one of the best ways of fami-
liarising the Eastern Ivish public with Irish that I
know of. Of course, either scheme must, for the mo-
ment, be in the air. In France the State supports the
Opera ;-and we have no immediate prospect of anything
of that sort at present. Nor do I entirely envy the lot
of the rich man who should step forward to endow Irish
Opera. He would scarce escape the fate of Orpheus at
the hands of musical experts. Meantime, we must put
up with what we have. And it seems to me that at
present the traditional taste for Opera is one of the
healthiest things in connection with the commercial
theatre. CHANEL.

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT,—XV.

ProTEsTANT Law AND Locrc.

ITHERTO, with two or three short digressions,

T have travelled on the same road with Protes-

tants of all denominations. -I now part company with

them. T hold, that we have not a particle of evidence

to prove that Erasmus Smith imposed on his Endow-

ment any trust other than those stated in the last

article, or appointed any beneficiaries, other than those
designated therein.

Protestants, on the other hand, kold that he imposed,
besides the trusts mentioned in the last article, a reli-
gious trust ; and by vivtue of this alleged religious trust,
they have violated, or left unfulfilled, nearly all, if not
all, the trusts mentioned in the last article; have ex-
cluded nearly®all, if not all the Beneficiaries designated
therein; and have given all the Endowment, with *u-
finitesimal exceptions, if with any, to persons who have
none of the qualifications of Beneficiaries, specially de-
fined by the founder. Certainly, the alleged religious

intention upon which such sweeping change has been
grounded, should rest on a solid foundation. Reason

234
and Law imperatively demand that the evidence proving
that intention, should put it beyond all doubt. I have
proved—and Protestants have admitted my proof—that
all the Catholic tenants’ children on all Erasmus Smith’s
Estates in Ireland, and all the Catholic tenants’ chil-
dren on all his Endowment Estates, and twenty “ other
poor children ** dwelling within two miles of his schools
at Drogheda, Galway, and Tipperary, are entitled to
free education, in all its grades, from his Endowment.
Reason, therefore, demands that before Protestants can
take away this clearly defined right from some 7,000
Catholic children, individually designated, so far as the
tenants’ childven on the Estates are concerned, and
from sixty “ other poor children,’”” in the neighbourhood
of the three Free Schools, by reason of an alleged Pro-
testant intention, imposed as a trust by Erasmus Smith,
they must prove that alleged Protestant intention by
irrefragable evidence. This is the demand of reason,
this is the demand of equity, this is the demand of
Chancery Law.

I shall now show that there is not a particle of evi-
dence to prove that Erasmus Smith imposed any such
religious trust, either Puritan or Episcopalian, on his
Endowment. But before showing this, I ask in a
general way, if Erasmus Smith did impose a religious
trust on his Endowment, what was it? Was it anti-
Episcopalian? or Episcopalian? The anti-Episcopalian
character was first imposed on the Endowment under
Cromwell ; the Episcopalian character was imposed on it
under Charles II. Did Erasmus Smith impose on his
Endowment of his own free will two religious characters
diametrically opposite? I shall prove that he was left
no choice in the matter; and that there is no tvidence
that he did.

I shall take two Canons, or Principles cf evidence, laid
down by Lord Justice Fitzgibbon. These are:—

1.—No intention is to be admitted as an intention of
Erasmus Smith of which we have not evidence.

2.—Anything which Erasmus Smith was compelled to
do by the laws of his day, is not evidence of his inten-
tion.

At a Public Sitting held in 1892, Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon, Rep. 1894, p. 8, said :— The question is, what
is the intention of which we have evidence?’’ When
I had quoted this principle against Lord Justice Fitz-
gibbon, to prove that we are not to admit any Protestant
intention in Erasmus Smith, because we had 70 evidence
of any such intention, he changed what he had said in
the Report of 1894, into—" The question is a very im-
portant one; what is the intention of which we have
evidence?’’ This is typical of the polemical methods of
Lord Justice Fitzgibbon.

But we, fortunately, have this first Canon laid down
as an incontestable principle of Chancery law in the de-
cision’of the House of Lords, given in August, 1904, on
the Free Church of Scotland. The majority of the Law
Lords have decided in that case, that no religious tenet
or principle is to be admitted, as a ground for transfer-
ring trust funds, except it is proved by evidence. All
the Lovds admitted that the Free Church of Scotland
had changed its doctrine by its union with the United
Presbyterian Church. The minority, Lords Mac-
naghten and Lindley, held that the General Assembly
of the Free Church had an innate power to change the
doctrine of that Church without changing the identity
of that Church, and thus had power to make the
change of doctrine caused by its union with the United
Presbyterian Church without losing its identity, and
that, subsequently, as the Church formed by the union
of the two Churches, was identical with the Free
Church, the property of the Frec Churches should pass

The five Law Lords who composed the majority held
that bofore the alleged power of the General Assembly
to chango the doctrine of the Free Church could ke
used as a basis for transferring the property of the Free
Church to the two united Churches, that alleged powenr
of changing doctrine should be proved by evidence, and
that there was no evidence of such power. They
grounded this decision on the decision of the House of
Lords of the case Craigdallic ». Aikman, on which Lord
Eldon said:—If it were distinctly intended that the
synod should direct the use of the property, that ought
to have beem matter of contract, and then the: Court
could act upon it; but there must be evidence of such
a contract, and here he could find none.” On the
same point Lord Davey says:—The right of the As-
sembly to impose any innovation from established dac-
trino on a dissentient minority, and the limits of such
right (if any) must be found in the constitutional
powers of that body, and must be provided by evi
dence.””  Appeals, 1904, p. 581.

Although the basis of the trust, determining the
beneficiaries in the Scotch Church case is different from
the basis of the trust determining the bencficiaries in the
Erasmus Smith case—in the former the basis of the
trust is a collection of religious tenets and principles,
in the latter it is the relation of tenancy with Erasmus
Smith and his Corporation, and proximity to the three
Schools—yet, the principle laid down by the Law Lords
in the Scotch case, is directly applicable to the
Erasmus Smith Endowment. For, as we have seen,
the children of all Erasmus Smith’s tenants, and of all
the tenants of his Corporation, and 60 “ other poor
children *’ within two miles of his three Free Schools,
are the beneficiaries entitled to free education from his
Endowment ; and before an alleged religious trust can
be made the causc of transferring the property from all
these, it must be proved by evidence, just as the majority
of the Law Lords required that the alleged power of
the General Assembly of the Free Church to change
doctrine, should be proved by evidence before it could
be made a cause, or ground, for transferring the pro-
perty of the Free Church. The first canon of evidence.
therefore, is a principle of Chancery Law established be-
yond all question. And no religious intention is to be
admitted in Erasmus Smith as a ground for transferring
property of which we have not evidence. Right reason,
also, as we have seen, demands this canon of evideunce.
For the right of the tenants’ children, and others, to
frec education from the Endowment has been clearly
defined, and clearly proved. And those rights cannot
be taken from the tenants and others without proof, at
least, as clear, and at least, as defined.

I now proceed to state the 2nd canon of evidence laid
down by Lord Justice Fitzgibbon. TGRSR (2E ==

In his “ Observations ou the Draft Scheme,” Rep.

bearing on the question is to be desived.  Proof of the

the Established Church, with fuller information as to
